Glossary About
Late Antiquity

Plotinus

c. 204 – 270 CE

Soul between the One and the sensible world: the founder of Neoplatonism on interiority, intellect, and the return.

Plotinus on Soul and Mind: a primary-source report

Since you asked for a report grounded in primary sources, I am taking “previous scholarship” in the ancient sense: the earlier philosophical and exegetical tradition that Plotinus is explicitly reading, appropriating, correcting, and sometimes rejecting. Porphyry describes Plotinus’s seminars as beginning from readings in Plato, Aristotle, and later commentaries; he also reports that Plotinus’s writings are saturated with unstated Stoic and Peripatetic material, especially Aristotle. Plotinus himself insists in Ennead V.1 that his basic doctrine is not a novelty but an exposition of older teaching. That self-positioning matters: Plotinus does not present himself as founding a new school by rupture, but as giving a more exact account of truths he thinks earlier philosophers glimpsed only partially.

A first clarification is essential. In Plotinus, “soul” and “mind” are not simply two English labels for the same inner faculty. Soul (psychē) is a hypostasis: the life-principle that mediates between intelligible reality and the sensible cosmos. Mind in the strong Plotinian sense is Nous, usually translated “Intellect”: the higher hypostasis in which being, thought, and intelligible form coincide. Ordinary human thinking belongs primarily to soul, especially to its discursive or reasoning activity; genuine noetic self-knowledge belongs to Intellect. Plotinian “psychology” is therefore inseparable from ontology: to ask what the soul is is already to ask where, in the structure of reality, life and cognition stand. The key texts are I.1, I.2, III.7, IV.3–4, IV.7, IV.8, V.1, and V.3.

The core thesis of Plotinus’s doctrine is straightforward, even if its ramifications are not: Soul is the direct image and activity of Intellect, and Intellect is the first determinate reality after the absolutely simple One. In V.1 Plotinus says that Soul is the “utterance” or expressed activity of Intellect, just as Intellect is the expressed activity of the One. Because Soul comes from Intellect, it is intellective in origin; because it is derivative, its knowing is weaker, more distributive, and more sequential. Intellect possesses its contents all at once; Soul unfolds them in a lower mode. This hierarchy is the framework within which every Plotinian claim about human mentality has to be read.

1. Soul as hypostasis: life, mediation, and world-order

Plotinus begins not from the human individual but from Soul as cosmic cause. In V.1 he asks every soul to remember that soul is the author of living things, the principle that gives life to animals, stars, and the ordered heaven itself. Soul is not a late by-product of matter; matter receives whatever order and vitality it has through soul. This is why Plotinus insists that soul is “more honorable” than the bodies it animates. The same tractate makes the World-Soul central: the cosmos is alive because soul has entered, illuminated, and ordered it.

At the same time, soul is not spatially distributed in the way bodies are. Plotinus repeatedly argues that each living thing lives by soul whole and entire, not by a detached fragment mechanically assigned to a bodily part. That thesis is developed in IV.2 and IV.3: bodies are divisible, but soul, though present throughout a body, is not cut into bodily pieces. This lets Plotinus say both that soul is one and that the plurality of souls belongs to a single psychic order. The Stanford entry usefully summarizes this by saying that souls form a “one-many” hypostasis; that is a fair description of what the primary texts are doing.

One of Plotinus’s boldest and most characteristic claims follows from this: strictly speaking, soul is not in body as water is in a jar or as form is in a spatial container. In IV.3 he rejects every ordinary model of presence—place, vessel, substrate, part-in-whole—and concludes that if we must speak metaphorically, body is in soul more truly than soul is in body. He explicitly praises Plato for putting body in soul, not soul in body. The image he prefers is light in air: the light pervades the air without being spatially parcelled out or materially mixed with it. This is a major refinement of the Timaeus, where Plato says that the Demiurge stretched soul through the cosmos and around it, making soul older than body and ruler over it. Plotinus radicalizes that Timaean priority into a general anti-spatial doctrine of psychic presence.

That same anti-spatial doctrine also lets Plotinus preserve the transcendence of the higher psychic powers. Some powers of soul are present for bodily operations such as sensation; others have no concern with body at all. In IV.3 he says there are soul-powers whose presence to body must simply be denied. The human problem is therefore not how an immaterial thing can be trapped in a place, but how a being whose nature exceeds body can nevertheless animate and govern a body without losing its own higher identity.

2. The human being: composite, “we,” and the undescended soul

Plotinus’s anthropology is layered. He distinguishes the pure soul, the “animate” or living composite produced when soul irradiates an organized body, and the higher human subject that presides over this composite. In I.1 he says that from body plus a light or irradiation given off by soul there arises a distinct animate principle in which sensation and bodily affections occur. But the true “we” stands above this animate level. The authentic human being is not identical with the psychophysical compound. This is why in I.1 and elsewhere Plotinus can speak of the “veritable” or “authentic” human being as something nobler than the merely ensouled organism.

That distinction explains why Plotinus tends to relocate many passions away from the pure soul as such. Fear, appetite, pain, and similar states belong primarily to the animate compound or to lower psychic phases operating with body. Soul may register and judge bodily impressions, but it is not simply identical with the field of passions. In IV.3 he compares bodily sensation to a shared task in which body is the passive instrument and soul the active interpreter. Memory, however, belongs properly to soul, not to the compound as such; and he rejects “seal in wax” models of psychic imprinting. Plotinus’s point is not that embodied life is unreal, but that one must distinguish levels of agency if one is to understand moral failure and inner conflict.

This layered anthropology culminates in perhaps the most famous Plotinian thesis about the soul: not all of it descends. In IV.8 Plotinus says that “something” of our soul remains continuously in the intelligible realm; in another passage he says that even when human souls descend, their higher part stays above the heavens. This is how he resolves the paradox announced in the opening of IV.8, where he describes his own recurrent experience of rising above body into a more divine mode of selfhood and then “descending” again to discursive reasoning. Our embodied life is real, but it is not the whole truth about what we are.

The doctrine of descent itself is carefully balanced. Plotinus refuses both a naïve celebration of embodiment and a crude doctrine that embodiment is sheer disaster. In IV.8 he says Plato seems to speak in two voices: in the Phaedo, Phaedrus, and cave imagery of the Republic embodiment looks like imprisonment, while in the Timaeus the cosmos is a blessed god animated for good reason. Plotinus’s solution is to distinguish the natural procession of soul into a lower order from an excessive or culpable immersion in that lower order. Soul has a legitimate mediating role in the sensible cosmos; what is blameworthy is not simply manifestation but forgetfulness and overidentification.

This is why he can say both that descent has a dimension of fault or “audacity” and that it also reveals powers that would otherwise remain latent. Soul’s entry into the sensible order can bring harm when it is overinvolved, but it also manifests the productive richness of the intelligible. The sensible world, on this view, is not a prison built by an evil power; it is the lower expression of an intelligible plenitude. That anti-dualistic insistence becomes crucial when Plotinus turns against anti-cosmic rivals.

3. Intellect and the structure of mind

If Soul is life unfolded, Intellect is life and thought in concentrated simultaneity. In V.1 and V.3 Plotinus describes Intellect as the intelligible world itself: the realm in which forms are not inert abstractions but living, active intellects. Intellect knows not by searching but by possessing. Its object is not external to it; thinker and thought are identical there. Because all its content is present at once, Intellect is outside temporal succession. This is why Plotinus so sharply distinguishes it from the soul’s discursive reason.

Soul, by contrast, deals with things one by one. Plotinus explicitly says that soul handles “thing after thing,” while Intellect contains all at once. In III.7 he adds that time is “a certain expanse of the life of the Soul”: temporality is not just an external container but the mode in which discursive psychic life unfolds. That is an important development beyond Plato’s Timaeus. Plato had described time as a moving image of eternity; Plotinus internalizes that relation by making temporal succession a characteristic of soul’s own lower, distributive mode of contemplating intelligible reality.

Human cognition therefore occupies an intermediate position. In V.3 Plotinus says the soul’s reasoning power judges the representations presented to it by sense, but it can also attend to impressions coming “from” Intellect. The same passage ties reasoning to reminiscence: wisdom emerges when new sensory material is fitted to older, more original intelligible content. The reasoning soul can do genuine intellectual work, but its characteristic mode is mediation, discrimination, and comparison. It is not yet the all-at-once self-presence of Nous.

Plotinus is especially careful on self-knowledge. He begins V.3 by arguing that true self-knowledge cannot be explained as one part of a compound examining another part; that would still be knowledge of something else. Genuine self-knowledge requires the coincidence of knower and known. That is why the strongest form of self-cognition belongs properly to Intellect. The soul’s discursive reason may know itself in a secondary sense, but only by drawing upon Intellect or by becoming aligned with it. Here Plotinus’s doctrine of mind becomes intensely reflexive: the soul knows itself most truly when it ceases to remain merely discursive and rises toward noetic identity.

This yields one of his most striking formulas: Intellect is “ours and not ours.” In V.3 Plotinus says we are not Intellect, strictly speaking; rather, we represent it through the highest reasoning power. The ordinary “we” is the soul at a midpoint between sensation below and Intellect above. Yet when we turn upward and are shaped by Intellect, we become in a qualified sense what we contemplate. Plotinus thus rejects both a flat identification of the human self with ordinary empirical consciousness and a flat identification of the self with pure Intellect. Human selfhood is stratified, and spiritual practice consists in learning which stratum to inhabit.

The same distinction explains why memory belongs to soul, not to Intellect. In IV.3 Plotinus says there is no memory in Intellect because memory presupposes before and after, whereas Intellect lives in an unbroken present. Memory belongs to the temporally articulated life of soul. This is not a minor point. It shows that Plotinus’s “mind” doctrine is not a single-faculty psychology but a carefully tiered account of representation, memory, reasoning, and noetic presence.

4. Ethical and soteriological implications

Plotinus’s doctrine of soul and mind is inseparable from an ethics of purification. In I.2 he reads Plato’s “likeness to God” as requiring more than civic virtue. Political virtue orders the passions; higher virtue purifies the soul from bodily domination and turns it toward its own act, namely intellection. He says plainly that the soul’s true good lies in devotion to Intellect, its kin. The basic ethical movement is therefore not moralism in the narrow sense but ontological realignment: the soul becomes good by reorienting itself toward the level from which it comes.

Plotinus here stands very close to the Phaedo and yet does something more systematic with it. Plato had argued that soul is more akin to the invisible, divine, unchanging realm than body is, and that philosophy trains the soul to separate itself from bodily distraction. Plotinus accepts this logic and integrates it into the hierarchy One–Intellect–Soul. Purification is not mere abstinence. It is the recovery of the soul’s proper center of gravity, so that the intellectual life hidden within it becomes operative.

At the culmination of V.3 Plotinus says that to know Intellect one must strip away identification with body and even with the lower soul, allowing the soul’s godlike phase to become an image and medium of Intellect. The rhetoric is ascetical, but the metaphysical point is precise: the soul does not create the noetic realm by effort; it becomes transparent to what has always already been above it and, in a qualified sense, within it. The return is therefore both epistemic and ontological.

5. Plotinus in dialogue with earlier authorities

Plato

Plato is Plotinus’s principal authority, but not in a merely repetitive way. Plotinus says in V.1 that the doctrine of the One, Intellect, and Soul is ancient and already attested by Plato; Porphyry likewise presents Plotinus’s whole project as one that expects readers to know Plato thoroughly. Yet Plotinus does not simply collect Platonic statements. He systematizes them into a hierarchy of hypostases and solves tensions between dialogues by means of conceptual distinctions that Plato himself did not state in that form.

From the Timaeus, Plotinus takes several foundational commitments: soul is older than body and rules it; cosmic order requires soul; the soul’s constitution somehow mediates the indivisible and the divisible; the cosmos is a living whole. What he develops further is the metaphysical precision of those claims. The Timaeus says soul is stretched through and around the cosmos; Plotinus turns that into the thesis that body is in soul and that soul’s presence is non-spatial. The Timaeus gives the ontological dignity of soul; Plotinus makes Soul a distinct hypostasis, the direct image of Intellect. The Timaeus also links time to the ordered cosmos; Plotinus deepens this into the claim that time is the serial life of soul.

From the Phaedrus Plotinus takes the soul’s immortality, self-motion, and the mythic logic of ascent and descent. But he refuses to leave the matter at the level of myth. In IV.8 he explicitly turns to Plato because Plato seems both to blame embodiment and to justify cosmic ensoulment. Plotinus’s answer is more articulated than Plato’s mythic presentation: descent is a natural expression of soul’s mediating fecundity, while culpable “descent” is really overidentification with the lower. The doctrine that the higher soul remains above is his way of preventing Platonic embodiment from becoming an absolute fall.

From the Phaedo Plotinus inherits the language of purification, the contrast between body’s changing sensible objects and the soul’s kinship with the invisible and intelligible, and the thought that philosophy is a kind of disentangling from bodily domination. But he rejects any inference that the sensible cosmos is therefore bad in itself. The purified soul must indeed turn away from bodily entanglement; still, the world remains a beautiful image of intelligible reality, not an evil fabrication. This is a characteristic Plotinian reconciliation of the Phaedo and the Timaeus.

From the Republic, especially the analogy of the Good and the sun, Plotinus draws the decisive move of placing the first principle beyond Intellect and even beyond being. Plato says the Good stands to mind and intelligible objects as the sun stands to sight and visible things, giving truth and knowability while surpassing them in dignity. Plotinus interprets that as authorizing a first principle above Intellect; Intellect, precisely because it is self-thinking and contains intelligible multiplicity, must be second. This is one of the places where Plotinus’s doctrine of mind depends directly on his doctrine of the One.

Aristotle and the Peripatetic tradition

Aristotle is Plotinus’s chief philosophical interlocutor after Plato. Porphyry says Aristotle’s Metaphysics is especially concentrated in Plotinus, and the Stanford account correctly emphasizes that many Plotinian discussions look like sustained dialogue with Aristotle and his interpreters. This is particularly true in psychology and in the doctrine of Intellect.

In relation to De Anima, Plotinus adopts some Aristotelian precision but rejects Aristotle’s standard definition if it is taken to make soul simply the inseparable actuality or form of an organic body. Aristotle defines soul as the first actuality of a natural organized body potentially alive. Plotinus replies in IV.7 that if soul were form in this strict hylomorphic sense, it would be divided when bodies divide, sleep would become inexplicable, conflict between reason and desire would disappear, and intellection would be impossible except by importing another principle from outside the composite. He is especially sharp on the point that the reasoning soul cannot be wholly inseparable from body.

That is not simple anti-Aristotelianism. Plotinus appropriates Aristotelian themes while relocating them within a Platonic hierarchy. The clearest case is Metaphysics XII. Aristotle’s God is self-thinking thought, whose actuality is life and contemplation. Plotinus takes the model of self-thinking intellect very seriously, but he relocates it: such self-thinking belongs to Intellect, not to the absolutely first principle. In V.1.9 he explicitly says Aristotle begins well by making the first transcendent and intellective, but then cancels its primacy by ascribing self-intellection to it. Why? Because for Plotinus self-thinking already implies intelligible articulation, and thus a level of one-many plurality proper to Nous, not to the utterly simple One.

So Plotinus’s relation to Aristotle is double. He embraces Aristotelian actuality-language, the thesis that thought and its object are one in genuine intellection, and the primacy of contemplation as highest life. But he rejects the Aristotelian collapse of first principle into thinking substance. Put differently: Plotinus is willing to be more Aristotelian than Aristotle about Intellect, but only because he is more radically Platonic about the first principle above Intellect.

Pythagorean, materialist, and Presocratic positions

Plotinus also positions himself against older psychologies that he thinks fail to respect soul’s ontological status. In IV.7 he rejects the Pythagorean view that soul is a harmony or accord of bodily parts. His objection is that harmony is derivative and depends on prior tuning, whereas soul is prior, governing, and often in conflict with the body. An accord does not rule the lyre; a musician does. Likewise, soul cannot be reduced to the bodily order it organizes.

He likewise rejects corporealist accounts of soul, including views that make soul body or pneuma. In IV.7 he argues that sensation, memory, and intellection cannot be explained by a bodily medium: if the sentient principle were body, impressions would function like seal-marks in wax, memory would fail, and awareness of unified objects would become impossible. Because bodies are extended and divisible, they cannot account for the partless grasp required in thought. Several of these targets look like the kinds of materialist psychologies associated with Stoic or quasi-Stoic theories, which fits Porphyry’s remark that Plotinus was constantly engaging concealed Stoic material.

At the level of intellectual ancestry, Plotinus also retroactively recruits the Presocratics. In V.1.8–9 he says Parmenides approached the identity of being and intellect, Anaxagoras posited a pure unmixed mind, Heraclitus recognized the flux of bodies and the eternity of the One, and Empedocles grasped a principle of unity alongside division. Yet he also says they lacked precision. This is typical Plotinus: he seeks continuity with earlier philosophy, but continuity on terms set by a more systematic metaphysics. The Presocratics become anticipations, not authorities equal to Plato.

Porphyry’s report about classroom reading makes the same point at the level of more recent predecessors. Plotinus was working in an environment saturated by Middle Platonist and Peripatetic commentary; Porphyry names figures such as Severus, Cronius, Numenius, Gaius, Atticus, Aspasius, Alexander, and Adrastus. But the point of these readings was not passive repetition. Plotinus used them as occasions for his own analyses of the aporiai. His doctrine of soul and mind is therefore best seen as a highly self-aware synthesis produced inside an exegetical culture, not outside it.

Anti-cosmic dualists

Plotinus’s psychology also develops through polemic against anti-cosmic dualism, especially the Gnostics addressed in II.9. Even without that treatise in front of us, IV.8 already shows the underlying issue. If embodiment is treated as pure exile from a hostile world, soul’s cosmic role becomes unintelligible. Plotinus refuses that conclusion. He maintains that the sensible world is a beautiful, ordered expression of intelligible power, and that soul’s presence in it is not in itself a fall into evil. Evil arises through material limitation and disordered attachment, not through the mere fact that there is a cosmos.

6. What Plotinus adds

Plotinus’s lasting contribution lies less in any isolated slogan than in the architecture he gives to inherited themes. He takes Plato’s scattered claims about the Good, Intellect, soul, purification, and cosmic animation and integrates them into a rigorous hierarchy of hypostases. He takes Aristotle’s account of self-thinking actuality and relocates it at the level of Nous. He rejects bodily and harmony theories of soul because they cannot explain unified awareness, memory, intellect, or moral conflict. He solves the Platonic problem of embodiment by distinguishing legitimate procession from culpable immersion and by making the higher soul permanently undescended. And he gives the most developed ancient account of the difference between discursive mind and noetic mind by tying the former to temporal succession and the latter to eternal simultaneity.

The result is a conception of the human being as ontologically stratified. We are not merely bodies, not merely the animate compound, not even merely discursive reason. We are a “double person,” capable of living downward into representation, passion, and temporal succession or upward into noetic self-presence. That is why Plotinus’s doctrine of soul and mind is at once metaphysical, epistemological, and ethical. To know what soul is requires knowing where it comes from; to know what mind is requires distinguishing reasoning from Intellect; and to become what one truly is requires conversion toward the level of reality already secretly present above the empirical self.

In that sense, Plotinus does not merely inherit earlier positions. He reorders them. Plato gives him the grand orientation, Aristotle gives him analytic pressure and noetic vocabulary, the Presocratics give him ancient anticipations, and rival psychologies give him argumentative targets. What emerges is a doctrine in which soul is the living image of Intellect, Intellect is the self-knowing fullness of being, and the human task is to discover that our deepest identity is neither exhausted by embodiment nor reducible to discursive thought.
